transformation_type: skeletal rearrangement of alpha-halo ketones to carboxylic-acid derivatives
common_conditions: treat alpha-halo ketone with base in the presence of an external nucleophile to induce rearrangement and capture of the product carboxyl derivative
description_short: Base-promoted rearrangement of alpha-halo ketones that proceeds through cyclopropanone-like intermediates to give rearranged carboxylic-acid derivatives or contracted rings.

transformation_type: assembly of substituted furans from beta-keto esters and alpha-halo carbonyls
mechanism_type: enolate addition followed by intramolecular substitution and dehydration
common_conditions: generate beta-keto ester enolate, react with alpha-halo carbonyl compound, then dehydrate the resulting dihydrofuranol or 1,4-dicarbonyl intermediate to the furan
description_short: Base-mediated condensation that converts beta-keto esters and alpha-halocarbonyl compounds into substituted furans, often via dihydrofuranol intermediates.

transformation_type: Lewis-acid-promoted conversion of glycals or enol ethers to unsaturated glycosides or cyclohexanones
mechanism_type: allyloxycarbenium formation with nucleophilic capture or ketoaldehyde cyclization
common_conditions: activate unsaturated carbohydrate or enol ether with Lewis acid, then trap the allyloxycarbenium with a nucleophile or allow rearrangement to a cyclohexanone
synonym_names: Ferrier rearrangement|Type I Ferrier reaction|Type II Ferrier rearrangement
description_short: Lewis-acid-promoted rearrangement chemistry of unsaturated carbohydrates that affords 2,3-unsaturated glycosides or highly substituted cyclohexanones.

mechanism_type: SN2 displacement driven by precipitation of inorganic halide salts
common_conditions: treat alkyl chloride, bromide, mesylate, or tosylate with sodium iodide or lithium bromide in a polar aprotic solvent to exchange the leaving group
description_short: Classical SN2 halogen-exchange reaction in which sodium iodide or related salts convert alkyl halides or sulfonates into the corresponding iodides or bromides.

mechanism_type: ene-hydrazine rearrangement followed by cyclization to indoles
common_conditions: form arylhydrazone from ketone or aldehyde, then heat under acidic conditions to induce Fischer indolization to a substituted indole
description_short: Acid-catalyzed conversion of arylhydrazones into substituted indoles through tautomerization, sigmatropic rearrangement, and ring closure.
